answer:
**Yama Smṛti does not contain a precise rule specifying purification rites after touching a dog.**

The text discusses general purification methods (e.g., bathing after contact with impure substances,[^1] or water's purifying qualities[^2]), penances for various sins and contacts,[^3] and dogs in other contexts: dogs' teeth metaphorically for ancestors of a Brahmin cohabiting with a Shudra woman,[^4] shunning dogs (along with *vṛṣalīpatis*) during *śrāddha* rites,[^5] and marking a slanderer's face with a dog's foot as punishment.[^6] However, no verse directly addresses purification specifically after touching a dog.

This topic appears in other Smṛtis (e.g., Manusmṛti on food touched by dogs[^7]), but Yama Smṛti (2 chapters, ~4,467 words) lacks it.[^8]

[^1]: Yama Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-65c739](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/#p-65c739).
[^2]: Yama Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-6b33ac](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/#p-6b33ac).
[^3]: Yama Smṛti, Chapter 1, [citation_url](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/).
[^4]: Yama Smṛti, Chapter 2, [p-199112](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/#p-199112).
[^5]: Yama Smṛti, Chapter 2, [p-bd59a3](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/#p-bd59a3).
[^6]: Yama Smṛti, Chapter 2, [p-cc700d](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/#p-cc700d).
[^7]: Manusmṛti, Chapter 4, [p-058068](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-058068) (food not touched by a dog).
[^8]: Yama Smṛti work, [citation_url](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/); Chapters 1-2 fully read.
